---
title: "Client Report - Flights: Column Aggregation"
subtitle: "Unit 2 Task 2"
author: "Sydney Ahlstrom Sanders"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import requests #new for reading in JSON
import io       #new for reading in JSON
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))
```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Which airport has the worst delays?__ Describe the metric you chose, and why you chose it to determine the “worst” airport. Your answer should include a summary table that lists (for each airport) the total number of flights, total number of delayed flights, proportion of delayed flights, and average delay time in hours. 

_I chose to use the proportion of delayed flights as the metric because it normalizes any differences in the total number of flights across airports, not discriminating between airports with very different flight volumes. This allows for a fair comparison of delay performance across airports._

```{python}
summary_table = (
    df.filter(
        ~pl.col("num_of_delays_total").cast(pl.Utf8).is_in(["-999", "1500+"])
    )
    .with_columns([
        pl.col("num_of_delays_total").cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("num_of_flights_total").cast(pl.Int64),
        pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").cast(pl.Float64)
    ])
    .group_by("airport_code")
    .agg([
        pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum().alias("total_flights"),
        pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum().alias("total_delayed"),
        ((pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").sum() / 60) / pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum()).alias("avg_delay_hours")
    ])
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("total_delayed") / pl.col("total_flights")).alias("prop_delayed")
    )
    .sort("prop_delayed", descending=True)
)

print(summary_table)
```

## QUESTION 2

__Create a barplot showing the proportion of all flights that are delayed at each airport. Describe what you learn from this graph.__  

_I found that San Francisco (SFO) and Chicago O'Hare (ORD) have the highest proportion of delayed flights, indicating they are the worst airports in terms of flight delays. In San Fransisco this is due to heavy fog generally, and in Chicago there is often harsh winter storms._

```{python}
p = (
    ggplot(summary_table, aes(x='airport_code', y='prop_delayed', fill='airport_code'))
    + geom_bar(stat='identity', show_legend=False)
    + scale_y_continuous(format='.0%') 
    + labs(
        title="Proportion of Delayed Flights by Airport",
        subtitle="Comparing the likelihood of a delay across major airports",
        x="Airport Code",
        y="Percentage of Flights Delayed"
    )
    + theme_minimal2()
)

p.show()
```
